text: This is Dr. Craig Rogers, Director of Renal Surgery at the Vatakuta Urology Institute at Henry Ford Hospital. In this video, we demonstrate steps for bowel mobilization.
The surgeon incises the white line of Toldt with curved monopolar scissors, reflecting the bowel using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques.
The bowel is repositioned to its original location, revealing the avascular plane developed between the posterior mesocolon and Gerota's fascia.
The fourth robotic arm grasps the anterior Girotis fascia, retracting the kidney anteriorly to aid in further bowel mobilization.
The surgeon delicately dissects the brighter yellow mesenteric fat from the paler fat of the Girotis fascia, revealing the distinct color contrast between these tissue layers.
The robotic grasping instrument is used to spread open the space between the mesentery and Girota's fascia, allowing for precise incision of the tissue while protecting the underlying structures.
The surgeon exposes the upper pole of the kidney by mobilizing the spleen or liver, revealing the anatomical structures surrounding the kidney.
The surgeon releases the splenic flexure, pancreas, and splenic attachments, enhancing the exposure of the renal hilum for further surgical access.
Care must be taken to avoid injury to the pancreas and splenic vessels.
The surgeon uses scissors opened like a hook to carefully dissect tissue, minimizing trauma to the underlying structures.
The surgeon releases the splenic attachments, enabling the spleen to be reflected medially, improving access to the renal hilum.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the spleen to avoid injury to the stomach, which is positioned behind the spleen and vulnerable during the procedure.
The assistant uses a long tip suction to provide counter traction, aiding in the release of the upper pole attachments of the kidney.
For right-sided tumors, the surgeon uses a locking grasper through a five millimeter subxiphoid port to perform passive liver retraction, securing the liver to the diaphragm.
In the next video, we demonstrate the identification of important anatomical landmarks while performing robotic partial nephrectomy.
